up.method: TOC + Updegraff cellulose assay; 4.9 g/L cellulose degraded over 14 days (average volumetric rate = 4.9/(14*24))
cited from Desvaux et al. ref [21]; flux at pH 6.4-7.0, NOT measured in this study

sec.method: HPLC; residual extracellular pyruvate accumulation in this mono-culture
HPLC; lactate is main product with acetate and ethanol at high cellulose (concentrations shown only in figures)

yields.note: ~24% of total carbon goes to CO2 (from carbon balance/TOC, consistent with prior model)

growth_records.cite: Salimi, Fahimeh et al. Characterizing metabolic interactions in a clostridial co-culture for consolidated bioprocessing. BMC Biotechnology (2013). DOI:10.1186/1472-6750-13-95. [PMC3827933]
growth_records.method: reported (qPCR-derived specific growth rate 1.23 day^-1, converted to per-h)
growth_records.snippet: qPCR data showed that cells reached a stationary growth phase after 9 days with a maximum cell density of 5x10^9 cell/mL and a specific growth rate of 1.23 day-1 ... 1.5 g/L of carbon was solubilized after 14 days, which is equivalent to 4.
growth_records.notes: mu conversion: 1.23 day^-1 / 24 = 0.05125 h^-1. doubling time td=ln2/1.23=0.5636 day=13.53 h. Volumetric cellulose uptake computed: 4.9 g/L / (14 d * 24 h) = 0.0146 g/L/h (average, not specific). Cellulose degradation flux 1.69-1.84 mmol/(g cell h) is CITED from ref [21] (Desvaux et al.), not measured here. Max cell density 5x10^9 cell/mL. Pyruvate 0.004 g/L residual concentration (not a rate). Anaerobic batch, 37C, pH 7.2, CM3 medium at 20 g/L cellulose.

growth_records.medium: Co-culture medium (20 g/L cellulose), pH-profile mono-culture

up.method: TOC; no cellulose solubilization observed under this pH profile

growth_records.cite: Salimi, Fahimeh et al. Characterizing metabolic interactions in a clostridial co-culture for consolidated bioprocessing. BMC Biotechnology (2013). DOI:10.1186/1472-6750-13-95. [PMC3827933]
growth_records.snippet: the mono-culture run under co-culture pH profile did not show any cellulose solubilization during the batch, and cells had a very low growth rate of 0.16 day-1 (87% decrease) compared to the mono-culture batch at pH of 7.2 ... We observed p
growth_records.notes: mu conversion: 0.16 day^-1 / 24 = 0.006667 h^-1. td=ln2/0.16=4.332 day=103.98 h. No cellulose degradation (acidic pH inhibits growth). Pyruvate 0.029 g/L residual. Degree of synergism DS=1.23/0.16=7.99 (co-culture vs this mono-culture). Anaerobic, 37C, pH 7.0->6.0.

growth_records.strain: C. cellulolyticum ATCC 35317 + C. acetobutylicum ATCC 824
growth_records.medium: Co-culture medium (20 g/L cellulose), experiment A (autoclaved iron)

up.method: TOC + carbon balance; observed maximum cellulose degradation rate; 82% of 20 g/L degraded after 28 days
HPLC; pyruvate taken up later in the run coinciding with butyrate formation (uptake of C. cellulolyticum-secreted pyruvate by C. acetobutylicum)
HPLC; lactate uptake observed, coincided with butanol formation
HPLC; detected only early (from pre-cultures), consumed within 24 h

sec.method: HPLC; butanol detected in co-culture batch experiments
HPLC; maximum pyruvate concentration, later taken up
HPLC; main fermentation products (ranges shown in Figure 5, values not in text)

yields.note: ~24% total carbon to CO2; 82% of 20 g/L cellulose degraded in 28 d

growth_records.cite: Salimi, Fahimeh et al. Characterizing metabolic interactions in a clostridial co-culture for consolidated bioprocessing. BMC Biotechnology (2013). DOI:10.1186/1472-6750-13-95. [PMC3827933]
growth_records.method: reported (qPCR; C. cellulolyticum growth rate comparable to mono-culture at pH 7.2, i.e. ~1.23 day^-1; specific value for co-culture not separately stated)
growth_records.snippet: about 82% of cellulose was degraded after 28 days in the co-culture experiment A ... observed maximum cellulose degradation rate of 0.108 g/(L. h) ... butanol concentration of 350 mg/L was detected in the co-culture batch experiments ... ma
growth_records.notes: Max cellulose degradation rate 0.108 g/(L h) is a measured volumetric rate (compared by authors to C. thermocellum 0.15 g/(L h) which is CITED from ref [26]). Butanol 350 mg/L and pyruvate max 0.17 g/L are peak concentrations, not rates. C. cellulolyticum reached 3x10^9 cell/mL (same as mono-culture max). Products: acetate, ethanol, lactate, butyrate, butanol. Anaerobic co-culture batch, 37C, pH 7.0->6.0, 20 g/L cellulose.

growth_records.strain: C. cellulolyticum ATCC 35317 + C. acetobutylicum ATCC 824
growth_records.medium: Co-culture medium (20 g/L cellulose), experiment B (filter-sterilized iron)

up.method: TOC + carbon balance; cellulose degradation declined by 38% compared to experiment A (i.e. ~62% of A); confirms co-culture was not iron-limited

sec.method: HPLC; same product spectrum as experiment A

growth_records.cite: Salimi, Fahimeh et al. Characterizing metabolic interactions in a clostridial co-culture for consolidated bioprocessing. BMC Biotechnology (2013). DOI:10.1186/1472-6750-13-95. [PMC3827933]
growth_records.method: reported (qPCR; C. cellulolyticum growth rate as high as experiment A)
growth_records.snippet: while the cellulolytic activity was high in the co-culture experiment B, and C. cellulolyticum growth rate was still as high as experiment A, but the cellulose degradation was declined by 38% compared to experiment A
growth_records.notes: Distinct condition from experiment A only by iron preparation (filter-sterilized vs autoclaved). C. cellulolyticum growth rate reported equal to experiment A (~1.23 day^-1 equivalent). Cellulose degradation 38% lower than A (no absolute rate given for B). Anaerobic co-culture batch, 37C, pH 6.0, 20 g/L cellulose.

up.method: D x 2.2 mM (all cellobiose consumed by C.c.)

sec.units: mol per mol cellobiose (2.7 in supernatant)

mfa.reaction_or_metabolite: end products digestible by other trophic groups

growth_records.cite: Miller, Lance D et al. Establishment and metabolic analysis of a model microbial community for understanding trophic and electron accepting interactions of subsurface anaerobic environments. BMC Microbiology (2010). DOI:10.1186/1471-2180-10-149. [PMC2906461]
growth_records.snippet: C. cellulolyticum was likely growth limited by the availability of cellobiose... produced little to no lactate... Each mole of cellobiose led to 2.7 moles acetate in the supernatant
growth_records.notes: Dominant fermenter in anaerobic tri-culture chemostat. mu=D=0.03/h at steady state (cellobiose-limited); doubling=23.1 h. Cellobiose (2.2 mM feed) fully consumed by C.c.: volumetric 0.03x2.2=0.066 mmol/L/h; per-gDW using modeled C.c. biomass 210 mg/L (Table 2): 0.066/0.210=0.314 mmol/gDW/h. Products 2.7 mol acetate + ~equimolar CO2 per mol cellobiose (Fig 5 model). H2 abundant in pure batch but <0.1 mM in tri-culture.

carbon_growth.citation: Miller, Lance D et al. Establishment and metabolic analysis of a model microbial community for understanding trophic and electron accepting interactions of subsurface anaerobic env
